steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The blank falls after "tree rings" and before "a single tree like this." The first clause ends with "tree rings" (a complete thought), and "a single tree like this, claims dendrochronologist Valerie Trouet, can tell the history of the world" is a second independent clause. The blank must correctly separate two independent clauses.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'rings. A': A period ends the first independent clause, and the capital A starts a new sentence containing the second. Correct punctuation. ✓
  • Choice B — 'rings; a': A semicolon can join two independent clauses, but "a" must remain lowercase since it continues the same sentence — yet the second clause begins with "a single tree," making "a" lowercase and correct grammatically. However, the actual answer choice uses lowercase "a," which is valid; but the passage completion with a semicolon produces an awkward, nonstandard reading here. ✗
  • Choice C — 'rings and a': "And" would join the two clauses as a compound sentence, but the two subjects ("over three millennia of climate data" and "a single tree") make the coordination semantically odd and the sentence unwieldy. ✗
  • Choice D — 'rings, a': A comma alone between two independent clauses is a comma splice. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
